title: Financial POA vs. Medical POA: Understanding the Difference
summary: These are often two separate documents with two different scopes, and a family sometimes has one without realizing they need the other.
category: Life Transitions III: Legal & Financial Shifts
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Financial POA vs. Medical POA: Understanding the Difference | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: These are often two separate documents with two different scopes, and a family sometimes has one without realizing they need the other.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Financial POA covers money and property decisions; medical POA (often called a health care proxy) covers medical decisions.
The same person can hold both, or different family members can hold each.
Having one doesn't automatically grant authority in the other's domain.

paragraphs: A financial power of attorney authorizes decisions about money, property, and financial accounts. A medical power of attorney — sometimes called a health care proxy or durable power of attorney for health care — authorizes medical decisions when someone can't make them for themselves. These are legally distinct, even when combined into one appointment of the same person.

Because they're separate, a family that only set up one may find themselves without needed authority in the other area exactly when a crisis makes it urgent — which is why reviewing both, together, is worth doing proactively.

nextSteps: Confirm whether both a financial and medical POA exist.
Check who is named for each, and whether that still makes sense.
Fill any gap with an elder law attorney before a crisis makes it more difficult.
disclaimer: This article is general information, not legal advice, and laws vary by state. The specific requirements, deadlines, and procedures described here may not apply exactly to your situation. Confirm all details with a licensed elder law attorney before acting, especially before signing, filing, or relying on any document.
